database_entries.industry_note: In manufacturing CMA, cost of materials consumed is split into Imported and Indigenous components based on the statutory disclosure in notes. This split is mandatory in CMA Form II.

database_entries.industry_note: In manufacturing CMA, stores and spares consumption is a separate manufacturing expense row. In services companies, this would go to Other Administrative Expenses.
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Stores consumed from Other Expenses note allocated to manufacturing by CA

database_entries.industry_note: In this manufacturing company, ALL employee benefit expenses go to CMA Wages (manufacturing). No admin salary split exists. This implies most/all employees are factory workers. In companies with significant admin staff, employee costs would be split between manufacturing wages and admin salaries.
database_entries.reasoning: Component 1 of 3 — Salaries and wages form bulk of CMA Wages. All employee costs to manufacturing because this is a factory-dominant operation (shoe sole manufacturing).

database_entries.industry_note: Conversion charges for job work and stitching are specific to footwear/shoe sole manufacturing. Stiching is a core production process outsourced to job workers. In other manufacturing, this row might be 'Sub-contracting charges'.
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Job work and stitching charges are outsourced production costs in shoe manufacturing

database_entries.industry_note: In manufacturing CMA, power and fuel is a separate manufacturing expense row. High power cost (213L on 5130L sales = 4.2%) reflects energy-intensive shoe sole manufacturing (moulding, heating). In services companies, power cost would go to admin expenses.
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Power and fuel from Other Expenses note allocated to manufacturing

database_entries.industry_note: Packing expenses are manufacturing costs in shoe sole production — products need specialized packaging. In services/trading, packing would go to selling expenses.
database_entries.reasoning: Component 1 of 4 — Packing expenses for finished shoe soles, allocated to manufacturing

database_entries.industry_note: Mould charges are highly specific to shoe sole manufacturing — moulds are used to shape soles. This line item is a strong industry signal for footwear manufacturing.
database_entries.reasoning: Component 2 of 4 — Mould charges for shoe sole production moulds

database_entries.industry_note: Royalty paid for technology/design rights from Italian parent (Suolificio brand). In manufacturing CMA this goes to manufacturing expenses. In other contexts, royalty might go to admin or be a separate CMA row.
database_entries.reasoning: Component 3 of 4 — Royalty for Italian shoe sole technology/design rights

database_entries.industry_note: In manufacturing CMA, FACTORY rent goes to manufacturing expenses (Row 16), while OFFICE rent would go to admin (Row 26). Here the entire rent is factory rent (leased from promoter company Chemcrown Exports Ltd). If company had separate office premises, rent would be split.
database_entries.reasoning: Component 4 of 4 — Factory rent (entire premises leased from Chemcrown Exports Ltd, a promoter company). All rent allocated to manufacturing because the premises are factory/production facilities.

database_entries.industry_note: In this manufacturing CMA, ALL depreciation goes to manufacturing expenses (Row 21). No admin depreciation split. This is common when fixed assets are predominantly factory plant & machinery. In companies with significant office/IT assets, depreciation would be split: P&M depreciation to manufacturing, office equipment/furniture/vehicles to admin.
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Entire depreciation charge to manufacturing. The fixed asset base is dominantly factory P&M (shoe sole moulding machines, plant). No separate admin depreciation line in this CMA.

database_entries.industry_note: Mould inventory surged from 2.40L to 227.90L in FY24 — a 95x increase. This represents significant capital locked in moulds, possibly for new product lines or customer-specific designs.
database_entries.reasoning: Component 2 of 2 — Closing moulds inventory. Massive buildup in FY24 (from 2.40L opening to 227.90L) is a key credit risk observation.

database_entries.raw_text: Rates and taxes (excluding taxes on income)
database_entries.industry_note: In manufacturing CMA, this row contains ONLY rates and taxes (not rent). Rent went to manufacturing expenses (Row 16). The CMA row label says 'Rent, Rates and Taxes' but contains only rates/taxes. This is because factory rent is manufacturing, and the company has no separate office rent.
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Only rates and taxes component. Rent was separately allocated to manufacturing (Row 16).

Approximate match — CMA 'Bad Debts' row (13.93L) maps to Note 25(h) 'Sundries' (13.99L). The 0.06L difference is rounding. Despite the CMA label, this is sundry admin expenses, not bad debts. The actual bad debts (9.33L) are in CMA Row 38.

Note 25(h) 'Bad Debts Written off' = 9.33L goes to CMA Row 38 instead. The CA appears to have used the 'Bad Debts' CMA label as a catch-all for sundry/miscellaneous admin items.

database_entries.reasoning: FY23 Direct match — Bad Debts Written off 2.49L from Note 25(h). In FY23, this row correctly maps to actual bad debts written off (unlike FY24 where sundries were mapped here).

database_entries.industry_note: Freight outward is a selling expense. In FY25, the CA split it out to CMA Row 14 (Freight and Transportation Charges under manufacturing). The FY23/FY24 treatment of putting freight in admin is unusual for manufacturing — typically freight is manufacturing or a separate selling row.

Component 2 of 9 — Insurance premium (covers factory, stock, transit). Not split between mfg/admin.

Direct match — Deferred tax charge for FY24. Tax impact of differential WDV of fixed assets creates DTL. No current tax as company has accumulated losses/tax credits.

Direct match — Deferred Tax Asset created in FY25 (credit to P&L = reduces tax expense). This reversal from DTL to DTA boosted PAT by 30.26L. PBT was only 67.35L, so DTA represents ~45% of reported PAT.

database_entries.section: Short-term Borrowings - Loans payable on demand
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Cash Credit facility from IDBI Bank. Secured by hypothecation of current assets + collateral charge on P&M + 2nd charge on factory land (leased) + personal guarantee of director + corporate guarantee of Chemcrown Exports Ltd.
database_entries.validation_issue: CMA field says 'Indian Bank' but actual lender is IDBI Bank per Note 8. The CMA template label was not updated to reflect the actual banker.

database_entries.raw_text: Current maturities of long term debts - for GECL Loan from IDBI - Repayable within one year
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — GECL (Government Emergency Credit Line) loan from IDBI, portion repayable within 1 year. Secured under NCGTC/CGTMSE guarantee.
Direct match — GECL term loan balance repayable beyond 1 year. Declining: FY23=136.01, FY24=28.16, FY25=nil — loan being repaid.

database_entries.industry_note: Unsecured promoter loans classified as 'Quasi Equity' by the bank in CMA. This is a bank credit assessment practice: subordinated promoter loans that function like equity for solvency analysis. Treated as part of Tangible Net Worth in TOL/TNW ratio. Growing: FY23=nil, FY24=594.81, FY25=1000.00 — indicates promoter infusion to shore up net worth.
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Unsecured loan from Note 5 classified as Quasi Equity in CMA. This likely represents loans from promoters/directors (Swati Bhaiya per related party disclosures) treated as quasi-equity for credit assessment.

database_entries.raw_text: Payable as per agreement for supply of goods & others - Suolificio Squadroni srl

database_entries.reasoning: Component 4 of 5 — Long-term gratuity provision included in CMA unsecured LT debt. Note: Auditor qualification says AS-15 not fully complied.

database_entries.industry_note: CMA reclassification of trade payables between current and non-current is a bank credit assessment decision. The CA moved ~105L of aged/disputed trade payables to non-current, which is conservative for credit assessment but distorts the current ratio comparison with audited financials.
database_entries.reasoning: Component 5 of 5 — Trade payables reclassified by CA from current liabilities to non-current in CMA. Likely capital goods creditors with long settlement cycles.
database_entries.validation_issue: ~105L of trade payables (likely capital goods creditors per Note 9 breakdown: 'Others for Capital goods = 10,499') reclassified from current to non-current in CMA. This INFLATES the current ratio (CMA 1.10 vs audited 1.40 for FY24). Verified: PDF trade payables (2081.16) - CMA Row 85 (1976.17) = 104.99.

database_entries.cma_field_name: Domestic Receivables ( including bills purchased and discounted)
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Trade receivables less than 6 months old per ageing schedule in Note 14. Amount = 1,10,769.76 in '000 = 1107.70L.

database_entries.industry_note: In CMA Form III, debtors outstanding >6 months are reclassified from current assets to non-current assets. This is a CMA-specific treatment (not per accounting standards) designed to give banks a conservative view of working capital. This is why CMA current ratio (1.10) differs significantly from audited ratio (1.40) for FY24.
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Debtors >6 months = Total receivables (2335.67) - <6 months (1107.70) = 1227.97. This is a CRITICAL item — includes Rs.491L outstanding >5 years per audit qualification.
Direct match — Cash on hand from Note 15 = 1,811.07 in '000 = 18.11L
Direct match — Bank current account balance
database_entries.validation_issue: Note 15 shows bank balances = 799.51 in '000 = 8.00L (rounded from 7.995). CMA shows exactly 8.00L.

database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Margin money deposits (likely against bank guarantees/LC facilities) from Note 15 = 5,427.73 in '000 = 54.28L

database_entries.raw_text: Trade receivables - More than 6 months (CMA reclassified)
database_entries.industry_note: This is the SAME amount as CMA Row 70 — debtors >6 months shown under current assets in audited BS but reclassified to non-current assets in CMA Form III. This dual presentation is standard CMA practice to segregate 'slow' debtors from working capital computation (MPBF calculation).
database_entries.reasoning: CMA Form III presentation — Debtors >6M reclassified from current to non-current for bank credit assessment. Same amount as Row 70.

database_entries.reasoning: Trade payables for goods — net of ~105L reclassified to non-current. Includes MSME (9.65L) and major portion of Others. The reclassified portion likely represents capital goods creditors with long payment terms.

database_entries.industry_note: In FY25, the CA reclassified freight outward from Selling Expenses (admin Row 29 in FY23/24) to a separate Manufacturing row (Row 14). This is more appropriate for a manufacturing CMA as outward freight is a distribution cost directly tied to sales volume. The FY23/24 CA had kept it in admin.
database_entries.reasoning: FY25 only — Freight outward moved from admin (Row 29 in FY24) to manufacturing (Row 14 in FY25). Reflects more accurate CMA allocation.

database_entries.industry_note: In FY25, the CA split repairs between manufacturing (Row 17: 25.15L) and admin (Row 30: 34.92L). Total 60.07L. In FY24, all 43.99L of repairs went to admin (Row 30). The FY25 approach is more accurate: P&M repairs are manufacturing, building/other repairs are admin.
database_entries.reasoning: FY25 only — Repairs split introduced. P&M and factory building repairs allocated to manufacturing.

database_entries.cma_field_name: Interest on Fixed Loans / Term loans
database_entries.reasoning: FY25 only — Interest on term loans separately identified (7.37L). In FY23/24 this was merged into Row 34 (WC interest) as the GECL loan interest was relatively small.
